AutoXplainR 0.2.0
  • Reference
    • Your First Model with AutoXplainR
  • Changelog

AutoXplainR: guided model fitting, evaluation, and explanation

Source: R/AutoXplainR.R
AutoXplainR-package.Rd

AutoXplainR gives a first-time modeler one safe path from a data frame to a held-out evaluation and plain-language explanation. An explicit local mode tunes a portfolio spanning linear, regularized, additive, tree, forest, boosting, neural, kernel, nearest-neighbor, and piecewise-linear behavior using only the training portion before the final holdout is scored. Its evidence layer compares what viable model families learned, then stress-tests explanations with repeated permutation importance, dependence diagnostics, accumulated local effects, and reproducible provenance. H2O AutoML, Plotly, and remote narratives are optional integrations rather than startup requirements.

Primary workflow

  1. Call autoxplain() with a data frame and target column.

  2. Read the baseline comparison and held-out metrics.

  3. Use model_set = "tuned" and tuning_results() when automatic model selection is needed without tuning on the final evaluation rows.

  4. Preserve the fitted patterns and limitations with render_model_report().

  5. Convert the result with as_explainers() to investigate an effect with explain_effect() or run audit_explanations() for advanced checks.

Interpretation boundaries

Permutation importance describes reliance of a fitted model on evaluation data. It does not establish causality. Intervals reported by the default estimator describe Monte Carlo variation across permutations, not sampling uncertainty in a target population. Audit grades are transparent heuristics, not safety, fairness, statistical, or regulatory certification.

References

Fisher A, Rudin C, Dominici F (2019). "All Models are Wrong, but Many are Useful: Learning a Variable's Importance by Studying an Entire Class of Prediction Models Simultaneously." Journal of Machine Learning Research, 20(177), 1–81.

Apley DW, Zhu J (2020). "Visualizing the Effects of Predictor Variables in Black Box Supervised Learning Models." Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B, 82(4), 1059–1086.
